  • Hide All Code

HUGEN 2072 Project 8

Instructions

Below is a guided tutorial to perform some advanced analyses building upon the results from project 7’s GWAS of height. Some code has been provided to you and some you will need to write yourself.

Project 8 has three components:

  • Scripting/coding: read through this file and complete the pipeline by contributing your code where indicated.

  • Narrative: knit the completed .Rmd to a code-integrated HTML report of your QC process and decisions.

    • Your report must answer all the questions and perform all the tasks indicated in the instructions/prompts given for each step.

    • You must submit a successfully-knitted HTML file and the .Rmd in order to earn a passing grade for this project.

    • Set up your report to flow like an actual document you might show your supervisor to summarize the project, emphasizing what was done at each step rather than the code used to do it

    • Submit your .Rmd and HTML to Canvas

  • Presentation: you will make a 10-min (maximum) recording of yourself with Panopto in which you summarize this GWAS. Instructions will given on Canvas.

In each part of this project you will implement one analysis strategy we learned in class (some steps completed for you).

Since the code below is incomplete, some of the R code chunks are set to eval=FALSE so that knitting to HTML will succeed. You will need to change them to eval=TRUE as you progress so that your knitted document shows the results of running each chunk.

It is expected that you will copy this file somewhere to your directory on the CRC cluster so that you can use the RStudio Server, where all the packages you need have been installed already.

To standardize the starting point of this project, I have provided the necessary results from project 7, so please use those instead of any of your generated files.

Part 0 - Setup Workspace and Load Sample Data

First, load the packages we’ll be using.

# Load the required packages for the project (Install them if necessary)

library("SeqArray", quietly = TRUE)
library("SeqVarTools", quietly = TRUE)
library("GENESIS", quietly = TRUE)
library("locuszoomr", quietly = TRUE)
library("EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86", quietly = TRUE)
library("rsq", quietly = TRUE)
library("EasyQC", quietly = TRUE)
library("qqman", quietly = TRUE)
library("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)
library("ggforce", quietly = TRUE)


# Close any open GDS files before proceeding (think of this as starting off with a 'blank slate')

showfile.gds(closeall = T, verbose = T)

All of the files you will need are located on the cluster in /ix1/hugen2072_2025s/p8/.

# path to folder for project
shared_folder <- "/ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/"

Part 1 - Evaluate GWAS hits

A version of the GWAS from project 7 has been provided for you in the file p8_study1_gwas_results.txt.

The columns here are:

  • variant.id: the variant ID from the GDS file

  • chr: chromosome

  • pos: position (hg38)

  • effect_allele: allele coded additively for GWAS

  • other_allele: the other allele at the variant (non-effect allele)

  • imp_r2: imputation quality r-squared

  • freq: the frequency of the effect allele

  • genotyped: TRUE if variant was genotyped, FALSE if it was imputed

  • n.obs: sample size with non-missing data at that variant

  • MAC: minor allele count

  • Score, Score.SE, Score.Stat, Score.pval, Est, Est.SE, and PVE: columns from assocTestSingle() run.

  • strand: strand indicator

# read in results from GWAS 1
gwas1 <- read_table(paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_gwas_results.txt"))
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   variant.id = col_double(),
##   chr = col_double(),
##   pos = col_double(),
##   effect_allele = col_character(),
##   other_allele = col_character(),
##   imp_r2 = col_double(),
##   freq = col_double(),
##   genotyped = col_logical(),
##   n.obs = col_double(),
##   MAC = col_double(),
##   Score = col_double(),
##   Score.SE = col_double(),
##   Score.Stat = col_double(),
##   Score.pval = col_double(),
##   Est = col_double(),
##   Est.SE = col_double(),
##   PVE = col_double(),
##   strand = col_character()
## )
gwas1
## # A tibble: 104,466 × 18
##    variant.id   chr     pos effect_allele other_allele imp_r2   freq genotyped
##         <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>         <chr>         <dbl>  <dbl> <lgl>    
##  1         21     1  891887 T             C             0.985 0.895  FALSE    
##  2         28     1  904493 T             C             1     0.356  FALSE    
##  3         30     1  911085 T             C             0.990 0.195  FALSE    
##  4         54     1  993941 C             A             0.998 0.934  FALSE    
##  5         70     1 1027340 T             C             0.993 0.0974 FALSE    
##  6         73     1 1035075 A             G             0.995 0.0110 FALSE    
##  7         74     1 1036806 T             C             0.981 0.0129 FALSE    
##  8         80     1 1059011 T             G             0.998 0.773  FALSE    
##  9         88     1 1086657 T             C             0.992 0.588  FALSE    
## 10        114     1 1194540 A             G             0.999 0.104  FALSE    
## # ℹ 104,456 more rows
## # ℹ 10 more variables: n.obs <dbl>, MAC <dbl>, Score <dbl>, Score.SE <dbl>,
## #   Score.Stat <dbl>, Score.pval <dbl>, Est <dbl>, Est.SE <dbl>, PVE <dbl>,
## #   strand <chr>

Part 1 - Report

Top SNP

In the results file provided to you, which SNP has the most significant association with height? Report the GDS variant.id, chromosome, position, alleles, GWAS p-value, GWAS effect size, effect allele, effect allele frequency, and whether the SNP was genotyped or imputed.

# Your code goes here
most_sig1 = gwas1 %>%
  arrange(Score.pval)
  
most_sig1 = most_sig1[1,]
most_sig1
## # A tibble: 1 × 18
##   variant.id   chr      pos effect_allele other_allele imp_r2  freq genotyped
##        <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>         <chr>         <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>    
## 1     542766    19 28492903 T             C             1.000 0.126 FALSE    
## # ℹ 10 more variables: n.obs <dbl>, MAC <dbl>, Score <dbl>, Score.SE <dbl>,
## #   Score.Stat <dbl>, Score.pval <dbl>, Est <dbl>, Est.SE <dbl>, PVE <dbl>,
## #   strand <chr>

The most significant association was the imputed SNP 19:28492903:C>T (id of 542766). The GWAS effect size was ~0.88 (p-value: ~4.1e-06).

Sina plot

The code below gives you an example of how to merge in genotypes for a variant from the results with the sample annotation file.

Edit the code below for the top SNP you just identified.

# load sample annotation file
load(paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_sample_info.RData"), verbose = TRUE)
## Loading objects:
##   sample.info
head(pData(sample.info))
##   sample.id    sex pop   height
## 1   HG00096   male EUR 170.5518
## 2   HG00097 female EUR 155.1560
## 3   HG00099 female EUR 157.5750
## 4   HG00100 female EUR 151.2360
## 5   HG00101   male EUR 161.9721
## 6   HG00102 female EUR 148.3681
# open SeqArray GDS file of genetic data from study 1
gds <- seqOpen(paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_imputed_data.gds"))
gds
## Object of class "SeqVarGDSClass"
## File: /ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/p8_study1_imputed_data.gds (481.4M)
## +    [  ] *
## |--+ description   [  ] *
## |--+ sample.id   { Str8 2548 LZMA_ra(7.84%), 1.6K } *
## |--+ variant.id   { Int32 647629 LZMA_ra(6.24%), 157.9K } *
## |--+ position   { Int32 647629 LZMA_ra(48.6%), 1.2M } *
## |--+ chromosome   { Str8 647629 LZMA_ra(0.03%), 489B } *
## |--+ allele   { Str8 647629 LZMA_ra(18.1%), 526.3K } *
## |--+ genotype   [  ] *
## |  |--+ data   { Bit2 2x2548x647629 LZMA_ra(4.04%), 31.8M } *
## |  |--+ extra.index   { Int32 3x0 LZMA_ra, 18B } *
## |  \--+ extra   { Int16 0 LZMA_ra, 18B }
## |--+ phase   [  ]
## |  |--+ data   { Bit1 2548x647629 LZMA_ra(0.01%), 29.5K } *
## |  |--+ extra.index   { Int32 3x0 LZMA_ra, 18B } *
## |  \--+ extra   { Bit1 0 LZMA_ra, 18B }
## |--+ annotation   [  ]
## |  |--+ id   { Str8 647629 LZMA_ra(36.2%), 2.3M } *
## |  |--+ qual   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(0.02%), 533B } *
## |  |--+ filter   { Int32,factor 647629 LZMA_ra(0.02%), 533B } *
## |  |--+ info   [  ]
## |  |  |--+ AF   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(46.2%), 1.1M } *
## |  |  |--+ MAF   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(46.0%), 1.1M } *
## |  |  |--+ AVG_CS   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(22.8%), 577.6K } *
## |  |  |--+ R2   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(53.8%), 1.3M } *
## |  |  |--+ ER2   { Float32 647629 LZMA_ra(15.1%), 380.9K } *
## |  |  |--+ IMPUTED   { Bit1 647629 LZMA_ra(13.6%), 10.8K } *
## |  |  \--+ TYPED   { Bit1 647629 LZMA_ra(68.1%), 53.9K } *
## |  \--+ format   [  ]
## |     |--+ DS   [  ] *
## |     |  \--+ data   { Float32 2548x647629 LZMA_ra(1.42%), 89.2M } *
## |     |--+ GP   [  ] *
## |     |  \--+ data   { Float32 2548x1942887 LZMA_ra(1.23%), 232.3M } *
## |     \--+ HDS   [  ] *
## |        \--+ data   { Float32 2548x1295258 LZMA_ra(0.95%), 119.2M } *
## \--+ sample.annotation   [  ]
# Extract genotype for one variant
#  this code just does the first variant in the results file
# Change my_chr and my_variant to match your variant of interest

my_chr <- "19" # change this to the chromosome you want (keep as character)
my_variant <- 542766 # change this to the variant.id you want (keep as numeric)

# Filter gds file to chromosome of interest
seqSetFilterChrom(gds, include=my_chr)
## # of selected variants: 30,037
# Additionally filter to variant.id of interest
seqSetFilter(gds, variant.id=my_variant, verbose=TRUE, action = "intersect")
## # of selected variants: 1
# Get dosage of variant
geno <- altDosage(gds)

# Close gds file
seqClose(gds)

# Create a data object with genotypes
geno_df <- as_tibble(geno, rownames = "sample.id")
names(geno_df)[2] <- c("genotype")

# Merge with annotation file
annot_geno <- inner_join(pData(sample.info), geno_df, by=c("sample.id"))

# Examine genotype distribution
annot_geno %>% count(genotype)
##   genotype    n
## 1        0 1950
## 2        1  555
## 3        2   43

Create a sina plot that shows the raw values of height across genotype groups at this top variant.

Comment on whether you think the genotype model you specified is appropriate for this variant or not.

# Your code goes here

# let's make the genotype a factor to clean-up the plot
get_allele <- function(id, df, ref = TRUE) {
  allele = df %>% 
    filter(
      variant.id == id
    )
  if (ref) {
    allele = allele$other_allele
  } else {
    allele = allele$effect_allele
  }
  return (
    allele
  )
}

ref_allele = get_allele(542766, gwas1)
alt_allele = get_allele(542766, gwas1, FALSE)

annot_geno = annot_geno %>% 
  mutate(
    genotype_fct = factor(
      genotype,
      levels = c(
        0, 1, 2
      ),
      labels = c(
        paste0(ref_allele, ref_allele),
        paste0(ref_allele, alt_allele),
        paste0(alt_allele, alt_allele)
      )
    )
  )

height_by_geno = annot_geno %>% 
  group_by(genotype_fct) %>%
  summarize(sd = sd(height, na.rm=T),
  mean = mean(height, na.rm=T))

annot_geno %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  aes(
    x = genotype_fct,
    y = height
  ) +
  geom_sina(
    alpha = 0.2
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(
      x = genotype_fct,
      y = mean,
      ymin = mean - sd,
      ymax = mean + sd,
      colour = "red"
    ),
    data = height_by_geno,
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  xlab("genotype") +
  theme_minimal()

Given that the CC and CT genotypes are about the same and the TT genotype has a higher associated height, it would probably be best to use a recessive model.

LocusZoom plot

Edit the code below to create a LocusZoom plot centered around the SNP you just identified. Based on this plot you created, how confident do you feel that this signal is not a false positive? Explain.

########################################
### Code you need to edit
my_chr <- "19" # change this to the chromosome you want (keep as character)
my_variant <- 542766 # change this to the variant.id you want (keep as numeric)
my_lz_window <- c(28492903 - 250000, 28492903 + 250000) # change to be the positions for the window you want to plot (typically +/- 250 kb around your top SNP)
#######################################

# a function to calculate LD from a gds file for a given index variant
calculateLD <- function(gdsfile, chr, variant.id, ref.var=NULL, sample.id=NULL) {
    gds <- seqOpen(gdsfile)
    seqSetFilterChrom(gds, include=chr)
    seqSetFilter(gds, variant.id=variant.id, sample.id=sample.id, verbose=TRUE, action = "intersect")
    geno <- altDosage(gds)
    seqClose(gds)

    # correlations for just ref.var, or the entire matrix?
    if (is.null(ref.var)){
        geno.ref <- geno
    } else {
        geno.ref <- geno[,as.character(ref.var)]
    }

    # drop unnecessary dimensions (if ref.var is a single variant)
    r <- drop(cor(geno, geno.ref, use="pairwise.complete.obs"))
    
    ld <- as_tibble(r^2, rownames="variant.id") %>% 
      dplyr::rename(LD = value) %>% 
      mutate(variant.id = as.numeric(variant.id)) %>% 
      dplyr::select(variant.id, LD)
    
    return(ld)

}

# Subset results file to region of interest
gwas1_locus <- 
  gwas1 %>% 
  filter(chr == as.numeric(my_chr) & pos >= my_lz_window[1] & pos <= my_lz_window[2])

# calculate LD with variant
ld <- calculateLD(gdsfile = paste0(shared_folder, "p8_study1_imputed_data.gds"),
                  chr = my_chr,
                  variant.id = gwas1_locus$variant.id,
                  ref.var=my_variant) 
## # of selected variants: 30,037
## # of selected variants: 40
# merge LD back with gwas results
gwas1_locus <- inner_join(gwas1_locus, ld %>% dplyr::select(variant.id, LD), by = "variant.id")

# create a unique SNP identifier column
gwas1_locus <- 
  gwas1_locus %>% 
  mutate(snp_id = paste(chr, pos, other_allele, effect_allele, sep=":"))

# locuszoom plot
loc <- locus(data = gwas1_locus, 
             p = "Score.pval",
             xrange = my_lz_window, 
             seqname = my_chr,
             labs = "snp_id",
             LD = "LD",
             ens_db = "EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86")
## chromosome 19, position 28242903 to 28742903
## 40 SNPs/datapoints
summary(loc)
## Index SNP 19:28492903:C:T 
## Chromosome 19, position 28,242,903 to 28,742,903
## 40 SNPs/datapoints
## 11 gene transcripts
## 9 lincRNA, 1 processed_transcript, 1 transcribed_unprocessed_pseudogene 
## Ensembl version: 86 
## Organism: Homo sapiens 
## Genome build: GRCh38
locus_plot(loc)

I feel rather confident that this signal is not a false positive given the emptiness directly above and below it that may be accounted for by other variants in the location that may be missing from this data set which this SNP may interact with. Also, this SNP in particular lines up across multiple genes. While the variant itself does not reach genome-wide significance, it may still help account for height partially as part of a threshold model rather than something Mendelian.

Part 2 - PGS

Another study of height generated a polygenic score to predict height (https://www.pgscatalog.org/score/PGS002975/).

You have been provided with pre-harmonized data for the PGS scoring file p8_pgs002975_scores.txt and PLINK formatted data for study 1 p8_study1.bed|bim|fam.

Edit the template code below to point the output to your directory with the OUT_FOLDER variable.

Then run the code below. At the command line, load PLINK with module load plink/1.90b7.7, then run the PLINK command.

module load plink/1.90b7.7

# edit this to point to your directory 
OUT_FOLDER=$HOME/p8

CLASS_FOLDER=/ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/

plink --bfile $CLASS_FOLDER/p8_study1 \
--score  $CLASS_FOLDER/p8_pgs002975_scores.txt header sum \
--out $OUT_FOLDER/p8_study1_scored

Read the .profile file created by PLINK, which contains the values of the PGS and merge with the phenotypes from the annotation object.

# Your code goes here

load_and_merge_score = function(file, annot_data) {
  pgs = read.table(
    file,
    header = TRUE
  )
  print(nrow(annot_data))
  print(nrow(pgs))
  merged = annot_data %>% 
    inner_join( # use an inner join to ensure we have a 1:1 joining
      pgs %>% rename(sample.id = IID),
      by = "sample.id"
    )
  print(nrow(merged))
  return(
    list(
      pgs = pgs,
      merged = merged
    )
  )
}

pgs_merge_results = load_and_merge_score("p8_study1_scored.profile", annot_geno)
## [1] 2548
## [1] 2548
## [1] 2548
pgs_merged = pgs_merge_results$merged
pgs = pgs_merge_results$pgs

Part 2 - Report

How many SNPs were in the starting PGS scoring files were used here? How many variants matched the PLINK files for the imputed data?

Based on these results from above applying the PGS for height, evaluate the utility of this PGS in predicting height. Make sure you report the PGS p-value, phenotypic variance explained (both incremental and partial r2), and an effect size estimate.

# Your code goes here

nrow(pgs_merged)
## [1] 2548
orig_pgs = read.table(
  "/ix1/hugen2072-2026s/p8/p8_pgs002975_scores.txt"
)
nrow(
  orig_pgs
)
## [1] 182
get_pgs_summ_stats_by_pop = function(population, data) {
  
  pop_data = data %>% filter(pop == population)
  
  m_full <- lm(
    height ~ SCORESUM + factor(sex),
    data = pop_data
  )
  m_reduced <- lm(
    height ~ factor(sex),
    data = pop_data
  )
  
  summ_full = summary(m_full)
  summ_full
  
  summ_reduced = summary(m_reduced)
  summ_reduced
  
  incremental_r2 = summ_full$r.squared - summ_reduced$r.squared
  incremental_r2
  
  partial_r2 = rsq.partial(objF = m_full, objR = m_reduced)$partial.rsq
  partial_r2
  
  row = list(
      "Population" = population,
      "Effect Size" = summ_full$coefficients["SCORESUM", "Estimate"],
      "PGS P-value" = summ_full$coefficients["SCORESUM", "Pr(>|t|)"],
      "Full Model R2" = summ_full$r.squared,
      "Partial Model R2" = summ_reduced$r.squared,
      "Incremental R2" = incremental_r2,
      "Partial R2" = partial_r2
  )
  return(
    row
  )
}

# from the last project, move the EUR,AFR to EUR
pgs_merged_pop_corrected = pgs_merged %>% 
  mutate(
    pop = if_else(
      pop == "EUR,AFR",
      "EUR",
      pop
    )
  )

pops = pgs_merged_pop_corrected$pop %>% unique()

# generate the above scores over pop as a covariate

scores_over_pop = lapply(
  pops,
  get_pgs_summ_stats_by_pop, pgs_merged_pop_corrected
) %>% bind_rows()
scores_over_pop
## # A tibble: 5 × 7
##   Population `Effect Size` `PGS P-value` `Full Model R2` `Partial Model R2`
##   <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl>           <dbl>              <dbl>
## 1 EUR                2.36         0.0764           0.620              0.618
## 2 EAS                0.391        0.772            0.636              0.636
## 3 AMR                1.01         0.513            0.624              0.624
## 4 SAS               -0.493        0.688            0.643              0.643
## 5 AFR                0.583        0.485            0.648              0.647
## # ℹ 2 more variables: `Incremental R2` <dbl>, `Partial R2` <dbl>
# plot the pgs colored in male vs female
pgs_merged %>% 
  ggplot() +
  aes(
    x = SCORESUM,
    y = height,
    color = sex
  ) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()

# plot the distributions of the pgs
pgs_merged %>% 
  ggplot() +
  aes(
    x = SCORESUM
  ) +
  geom_density() +
  theme_minimal()

Starting number of SNPs: 182 Generated from PLINK: 2,548 Variants matched: 2,548

Looking at our distribution, most of our PGS are about -0.5 and are falling between -1 and 0. Overall, checking across the populations, it seems like PGS are helpful for predicting height across our different populations. None of the groups appear to reach a level of predictive significance. Our PGS are also stratified by sex as we can see from the pattern of the scatterplot. Overall, given the stratification by sex and overall lack of effect across populations, I would not say that adding PGS is very useful in this case.

Part 3 - File Harmonization Before Meta Analysis

Use the R package EasyQC to perform quality checks for the GWAS from project 7 and for the second GWAS of the height from the UK Biobank (which was already been run).

You can assume the genotypes in both studies have been cleaned (although we will investigate allele frequencies a little). EasyQC is primarily checking that the GWAS output isn’t nonsensical and that alleles are coded consistently between the two studies.

The following files are provided for this part:

  • p8_study1_gwas_results.txt - standardized results from the GWAS of height from project 7
  • p8_study2_gwas2_results.txt - results from a GWAS for height from the UK Biobank (positions are in hg38) (that have been downsampled for you)
  • p8_gwas1_gwas2.ecf - a configuration file for EasyQC
  • p8_snp_reference.txt - an annotation file containing population allele frequencies

The configuration file gwas1_gwas2.ecf is almost entirely set up for you. You only need to make a few changes. Copy the file gwas1_gwas2.ecf to your home directory.

  • First read through the file and the comments. The documentation (https://homepages.uni-regensburg.de/~wit59712/easyqc/EasyQC_9.0_Commands_140918_2.pdf) also explains how the program works.

  • Specify where to write the output with the --pathOut flag (near the top of the file). Replace the path with a location within your home directory.

In R, load the EasyQC package and run your configuration script with the command like EasyQC("p8_gwas1_gwas2.ecf"), supplying the path to your .ecf file. This will take a few minutes.

The output includes a log, report (with counts of the number of variants dropped for each cleaning step), some plots, some more files containing information about variants failing some of the filters, and the cleaned data (i.e., GWAS results with problematic variants removed).

Note: everything that you will need to move forward is created as output files. So, you can run the next code chunk once and then leave eval=FALSE for the chunk when you knit this file.

Also note that multiple runs of this script file does NOT overwrite the results, it names them with successful counters (e.g., CLEANED.study_1.gz CLEANED_study_1.1.gz). I recommend you only run this once, otherwise you will need to carefully edit the metal script in the next part to point to the correct files.

# Process the GWAS files with EasyQC
# change this to the path to your folder where you have your copy of the ecf file
EasyQC("p8_gwas1_gwas2.ecf")

Part 3 - Report

Read the cleaned individual GWAS results (CLEANED.study_1.gz and CLEANED.study_2.gz from EasyQC), and the EasyQC report file (p8_gwas1_gwas2.rep) into R.

Summarize the sample sizes for the two individual GWASs.

How many variants were in each of the two individual GWAS (after EasyQC filtering)?

According to the EasyQC report, how many variants were dropped from each?

How many were flagged for allele frequency discrepancies, for being duplicates, or for failing some other validity check?

# Your code goes here
s1 = read_table("CLEANED.study1.gz")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   cptid = col_time(format = ""),
##   CHR = col_double(),
##   POS = col_double(),
##   STRAND = col_character(),
##   EFFECT_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   OTHER_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   EAF = col_double(),
##   BETA = col_double(),
##   SE = col_double(),
##   PVAL = col_double(),
##   N = col_double(),
##   MAC = col_double()
## )
## Warning: 107522 parsing failures.
## row   col       expected       actual                file
##   1 cptid valid duration 10:100001702 'CLEANED.study1.gz'
##   2 cptid valid duration 10:100023971 'CLEANED.study1.gz'
##   3 cptid valid duration 10:100043964 'CLEANED.study1.gz'
##   4 cptid valid duration 10:100061627 'CLEANED.study1.gz'
##   5 cptid valid duration 10:100084120 'CLEANED.study1.gz'
## ... ..... .............. ............ ...................
## See problems(...) for more details.
nrow(s1)
## [1] 107531
s2 = read_table("CLEANED.study2.gz")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   cptid = col_time(format = ""),
##   CHR = col_double(),
##   POS = col_double(),
##   STRAND = col_character(),
##   EFFECT_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   OTHER_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   EAF = col_double(),
##   BETA = col_double(),
##   SE = col_double(),
##   PVAL = col_double(),
##   N = col_double(),
##   MAC = col_double()
## )
## Warning: 483292 parsing failures.
## row   col       expected       actual                file
##   1 cptid valid duration 10:100043964 'CLEANED.study2.gz'
##   2 cptid valid duration 10:100061627 'CLEANED.study2.gz'
##   3 cptid valid duration 10:100084120 'CLEANED.study2.gz'
##   4 cptid valid duration 10:100129035 'CLEANED.study2.gz'
##   5 cptid valid duration 10:100212531 'CLEANED.study2.gz'
## ... ..... .............. ............ ...................
## See problems(...) for more details.
nrow(s2)
## [1] 483354
easy_report = read_table("p8_gwas1_gwas2.rep")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   .default = col_double(),
##   fileInShortName = col_character()
## )
## ℹ Use `spec()` for the full column specifications.
# also gonna load the AA mismatch and AF check files because these column names are unclear for getting specifics
# going to keep what I have parsed out below though
aa1 = read_table("study1.AA.mismatch.txt")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   .default = col_double(),
##   cptid = col_time(format = ""),
##   EFFECT_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   OTHER_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   GENOTYPED = col_logical(),
##   STRAND = col_character(),
##   a1.ref = col_character(),
##   a2.ref = col_character()
## )
## ℹ Use `spec()` for the full column specifications.
## Warning: 6512 parsing failures.
## row   col       expected       actual                     file
##   1 cptid valid duration 10:100061627 'study1.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   2 cptid valid duration 10:100212531 'study1.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   3 cptid valid duration 10:100359702 'study1.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   4 cptid valid duration 10:100562143 'study1.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   5 cptid valid duration 10:101461365 'study1.AA.mismatch.txt'
## ... ..... .............. ............ ........................
## See problems(...) for more details.
aa2 = read_table("study2.AA.mismatch.txt")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   cptid = col_time(format = ""),
##   CHR = col_double(),
##   POS = col_double(),
##   OTHER_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   EFFECT_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   R2 = col_double(),
##   EAF = col_double(),
##   N = col_double(),
##   BETA = col_double(),
##   SE = col_double(),
##   STAT = col_double(),
##   PVAL = col_double(),
##   STRAND = col_character(),
##   MAC = col_double(),
##   a1.ref = col_character(),
##   a2.ref = col_character(),
##   a1_af.ref = col_double()
## )
## Warning: 4565 parsing failures.
## row   col       expected       actual                     file
##   1 cptid valid duration 10:100061627 'study2.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   2 cptid valid duration 10:100212531 'study2.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   3 cptid valid duration 10:100562143 'study2.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   4 cptid valid duration 10:101461365 'study2.AA.mismatch.txt'
##   5 cptid valid duration 10:101673991 'study2.AA.mismatch.txt'
## ... ..... .............. ............ ........................
## See problems(...) for more details.
af1 = read_table("study1.AFCHECK.outlier.txt")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   .default = col_double(),
##   cptid = col_time(format = ""),
##   EFFECT_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   OTHER_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   GENOTYPED = col_logical(),
##   STRAND = col_character(),
##   a1.ref = col_character(),
##   a2.ref = col_character()
## )
## ℹ Use `spec()` for the full column specifications.
## Warning: 936 parsing failures.
## row   col       expected       actual                         file
##   1 cptid valid duration 10:102518519 'study1.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   2 cptid valid duration 10:102518519 'study1.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   3 cptid valid duration 10:102518519 'study1.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   4 cptid valid duration 10:102518519 'study1.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   5 cptid valid duration 10:105967173 'study1.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
## ... ..... .............. ............ ............................
## See problems(...) for more details.
af2 = read_table("study2.AFCHECK.outlier.txt")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   cptid = col_time(format = ""),
##   CHR = col_double(),
##   POS = col_double(),
##   OTHER_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   EFFECT_ALLELE = col_character(),
##   R2 = col_double(),
##   EAF = col_double(),
##   N = col_double(),
##   BETA = col_double(),
##   SE = col_double(),
##   STAT = col_double(),
##   PVAL = col_double(),
##   STRAND = col_character(),
##   MAC = col_double(),
##   a1.ref = col_character(),
##   a2.ref = col_character(),
##   a1_af.ref = col_double()
## )
## Warning: 3730 parsing failures.
## row   col       expected       actual                         file
##   1 cptid valid duration 10:103514513 'study2.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   2 cptid valid duration 10:10528003  'study2.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   3 cptid valid duration 10:106220886 'study2.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   4 cptid valid duration 10:106456526 'study2.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
##   5 cptid valid duration 10:106991453 'study2.AFCHECK.outlier.txt'
## ... ..... .............. ............ ............................
## See problems(...) for more details.
extra = as.data.frame(
  list(
    "fileInShortName" = c("study1", "study2"),
    "Allele Mismatch" = c(nrow(aa1), nrow(aa2)),
    "Allele Freq Error" = c(nrow(af1), nrow(af2))
  )
)

# Why is this report so gross? ... these columns names are so unclear... 
# Why does nobody ever make their columns self documenting or easily parseable...

allele_checks = easy_report %>% 
  select(
    fileInShortName,
    matches("AA|allele")
  ) %>% 
  select(
      fileInShortName,
      matches("invalid|Missing")
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    `Allele Errors` = rowSums(
      across(
        where(is.numeric)
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>% 
  select(
    fileInShortName,
    `Allele Errors`
  )

other_checks = easy_report %>% 
  select(
    fileInShortName,
    !matches("AA|allele")
  ) %>% 
  select(
    fileInShortName,
    matches("invalid|Missing")
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    `Other Errors` = rowSums(
      across(
        where(is.numeric)
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>% 
  select(
    fileInShortName,
    `Other Errors`
  )

easy_report_results = easy_report %>% 
  select(
    fileInShortName,
    numVarIn,
    numVarOut,
    numDuplicates.cptid
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    numDropped = numVarIn - numVarOut
  ) %>% 
  left_join(
    extra,
    by = "fileInShortName"
  ) %>% 
  left_join(
    allele_checks,
    by = "fileInShortName"
  ) %>% 
  left_join(
    other_checks,
    by = "fileInShortName"
  )

easy_report_results
## # A tibble: 2 × 9
##   fileInShortName numVarIn numVarOut numDuplicates.cptid numDropped
##   <chr>              <dbl>     <dbl>               <dbl>      <dbl>
## 1 study2            488915    483354                7244       5561
## 2 study1            104466    107531                   1      -3065
## # ℹ 4 more variables: Allele.Mismatch <int>, Allele.Freq.Error <int>,
## #   `Allele Errors` <dbl>, `Other Errors` <dbl>

According to the report:

Study 1: 104,466 in; 107,531 out; -3065 “dropped”; 1 duplicated, 6512 mismatches; 936 frequency errors; 1 other error

Study 2: 488,915 in; 483,354 out; 5,561 dropped; 7,244 duplicated; 4,566 mismatches; 3,730 frequency errors; 33 other errors

Honestly, I’m not too confident in these results given the increase for study 1 or from my initial understanding of these output files.

Part 4 - Meta Analysis

Use METAL to meta-analyze the two GWAS results.

First, read the documentation to learn how to make a script that meta-analyzes the two studies (https://genome.sph.umich.edu/wiki/METAL_Documentation).

The configuration file p8_metal_script.txt has been provided. Read through this file and use the documentation for METAL at the link provided to make sense of what options have been used in this script.

Copy this script to your folder. Edit the file to provide the paths to the EasyQC output files CLEANED.study_1.gz and CLEANED.study_2.gz.

At the command line, load metal with module load metal/2020-05-05. To run the meta-analysis use the command metal p8_metal_script.txt. Make sure that you run this from the folder that contains your copy of p8_metal_script.txt.

The output will be a log file and a text file with the suffix “.TBL”, which contains a table of meta-analysis results.

module load metal/2020-05-05
metal p8_metal_script.txt

Note that multiple runs of this script file does NOT overwrite the results, it names them with successful counters (e.g., METAANALYSIS_1.TBL, METAANALYSIS2.TBL). Be sure you read in the results file you want to by editing the code below.

Read the meta-analysis results (the .TBL file) into R and make Manhattan/Q-Q plots for the meta-analysis results. Edit the code provided below to point to the results file in your directory. You will need to turn on this chunk prior to knitting by setting EVAL=TRUE.

# Your code goes here
metal_results = read_table("METAANALYSIS1.TBL")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   MarkerName = col_character(),
##   Allele1 = col_character(),
##   Allele2 = col_character(),
##   Effect = col_double(),
##   StdErr = col_double(),
##   `P-value` = col_double(),
##   Direction = col_character(),
##   HetISq = col_double(),
##   HetChiSq = col_double(),
##   HetDf = col_double(),
##   HetPVal = col_double()
## )
# clean the data a bit for qqman
metal_results$MarkerName <- sub(":ID", "", metal_results$MarkerName)
metal_results = metal_results %>%
  separate_wider_delim(
    MarkerName,
    delim = ":",
    names = c("chr", "pos"),
    cols_remove = FALSE
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    chr = as.integer(chr),
    pos = as.integer(pos)
  )

# manhattan
qqman::manhattan(
  metal_results,
  chr = "chr",
  bp = "pos",
  snp = "MarkerName",
  p = "P-value"
)

# qqplot
qq(metal_results$`P-value`)

We are seeing definitive inflation in these plots from the meta-analysis.

MetaAnalysis Helper - (c) 2007 - 2009 Goncalo Abecasis This version released on 2020-05-05

This program faciliates meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies.

Commonly used commands are listed below:

Options for describing input files …

SEPARATOR [WHITESPACE|COMMA|BOTH|TAB] (default = WHITESPACE)

COLUMNCOUNTING [STRICT|LENIENT] (default = ‘STRICT’)

MARKERLABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘MARKER’)

ALLELELABELS [LABEL1 LABEL2] (default = ‘ALLELE1’,‘ALLELE2’)

EFFECTLABEL [LABEL|log(LABEL)] (default = ‘EFFECT’)

FLIP

Options for filtering input files …

ADDFILTER [LABEL CONDITION VALUE] (example = ADDFILTER N > 10)

(available conditions are <, >, <=, >=, =, !=, IN)

REMOVEFILTERS

Options for sample size weighted meta-analysis …

WEIGHTLABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘N’)

PVALUELABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘PVALUE’)

DEFAULTWEIGHT [NUMBER] (default = 1.0)

MINWEIGHT [NUMBER] (default = 1.0)

Options for inverse variance weighted meta-analysis …

STDERRLABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘STDERR’)

SCHEME [SAMPLESIZE|STDERR] (default = SAMPLESIZE)

Options to enable tracking of allele frequencies …

AVERAGEFREQ [ON|OFF] (default = OFF)

MINMAXFREQ [ON|OFF] (default = OFF)

FREQLABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘FREQ’)

Options to enable tracking of user defined variables …

CUSTOMVARIABLE [VARNAME]

LABEL [VARNAME] AS [HEADER]

Options to enable tracking of chromosomes and positions …

TRACKPOSITIONS [ON|OFF] (default = OFF

CHROMOSOMELABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘CHROMOSOME’)

POSITIONLABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘POSITION’)

Options to enable explicit strand information …

USESTRAND [ON|OFF] (default = OFF)

STRANDLABEL [LABEL] (default = ‘STRAND’)

Automatic genomic control correction of input statistics …

GENOMICCONTROL ON|OFF|VALUE|LIST snps.txt

Options to account for samples overlap …

OVERLAP [ON|OFF] (default = OFF)

ZCUTOFF [NUMBER] (default = 1.0)

Options for general analysis control …

PROCESSFILE [FILENAME]

OUTFILE [PREFIX SUFFIX] (default = ‘METAANALYSIS’,‘.TBL’)

MAXWARNINGS [NUMBER] (default = 20)

VERBOSE [ON|OFF] (default = ‘OFF’)

LOGPVALUE [ON|OFF] (default = ‘OFF’)

EFFECT_PRINT_PRECISION [NUMBER] (default = ‘4’)

STDERR_PRINT_PRECISION [NUMBER] (default = ‘4’)

ANALYZE [HETEROGENEITY]

CLEAR

Options for general run control …

SOURCE [SCRIPTFILE]

RETURN

Part 4 - Report

Based on your reading of the metal script, what method of meta-analysis did we use?

Using the meta-analysis results you read in above, do the following:

Make a table summarizing effect directions across the two studies

How many variants are shared across the two studies (i.e., how many have a non-missing effect and p-value in BOTH studies)?

How many variants are unique to each study?

What percentage of variants present in both studies have the same effect direction in both studies?

For the most significant variant in the meta-analysis that was present in both studies, make a forest plot that shows the two individual study effect estimates and confidence intervals. Hint: you will need to read in the cleaned files from Easy QC for each study. Comment on whether the variant’s effects seems to be homogeneous (based on the forest plot).

NOTE: Metal returns Allele1 and Allele2 and the effects and directions it reports are for Allele1. If you want the opposite allele coding, you need to multiple the effect estimate by -1.

# Your code goes here

# variants across the two studies...according to the docs, DF would be number of studies -1, so 2-1= any column where this is 1
both_studies = metal_results %>% 
  filter(
    HetDf == 1
  ) %>% # capitalize the Alleles
  mutate(
    Allele1 = toupper(Allele1),
    Allele2 = toupper(Allele2)
  ) %>% # correct the effect size direction for later
  mutate(
    Effect = -Effect
  )

in_one_study = metal_results %>% 
  filter(
    HetDf < 1
  )
# add directions split by study for later
directions = strsplit(both_studies$Direction, split = "") %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  t()
rownames(directions) = NULL
colnames(directions) = c("Direction1", "Direction2")

both_studies = bind_cols(
  both_studies,
  directions
) %>% # swap the directions because these are based on Allele 1 from each study
  mutate(
    Direction = if_else(
      Effect < 0,
      "-",
      "+"
    ),
    Direction1 = if_else(
      Direction1 == "+",
      "-",
      "+"
    ),
    Direction2 = if_else(
      Direction2 == "+",
      "-",
      "+"
    )
  )

s1 = s1 %>% 
  mutate(
    MarkerName = paste0(
      CHR, ":", POS
    )
  )

s2 = s2 %>% 
  mutate(
    MarkerName = paste0(
      CHR, ":", POS
    )
  )

# shared
n_shared = nrow(both_studies)
print("Shared")
## [1] "Shared"
n_shared
## [1] 66385
# unique to a study
n_unique = nrow(metal_results) - n_shared
print("Unique")
## [1] "Unique"
n_unique
## [1] 453161
# get the number of variants unique to each study

in_study_1 = s1 %>% 
  select(MarkerName) %>% 
  unique() %>% 
  inner_join(
    in_one_study %>% 
      select(MarkerName) %>% 
      unique(),
    by = "MarkerName"
  )

print("S1")
## [1] "S1"
nrow(in_study_1)
## [1] 38093
in_study_2 = s2 %>% 
  select(MarkerName) %>% 
  unique() %>% 
  inner_join(
    in_one_study %>% 
      select(MarkerName) %>% 
      unique(),
    by = "MarkerName"
  )
print("S2")
## [1] "S2"
nrow(in_study_2)
## [1] 415095
# what perc have the same effect direction in both studies
both_studies = both_studies %>% 
  mutate(
    DirectionsMatch = Direction1 == Direction2
  )

(nrow(both_studies %>% filter(DirectionsMatch)) / n_shared) * 100
## [1] 50.23725
# summarize the effect directions across the two studies
dir_table = both_studies %>%
  select(
    Direction, Direction1, Direction2
  ) %>% 
  as.list()

get_str_count = function(str_list, str) {
  count = sum(unlist(str_list) == str)
  return(
    count
  )
}

pos_counts = lapply(dir_table, get_str_count, "+")
neg_counts = lapply(dir_table, get_str_count, "-")

dir_table = cbind(
  pos_counts,
  neg_counts
) %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  mutate(
    pos_counts = pos_counts %>% unlist(),
    neg_counts = neg_counts %>% unlist(),
  )
rownames(dir_table) = c(
  "Meta-Analysis",
  "Study 1",
  "Study 2"
)
dir_table
##               pos_counts neg_counts
## Meta-Analysis      33399      32986
## Study 1            33053      33332
## Study 2            33212      33173
# identify the top SNP across the two studies
top_snp = both_studies %>% 
  arrange(
    `P-value`
  )
top_snp = top_snp[1,]
top_snp
## # A tibble: 1 × 16
##     chr      pos MarkerName  Allele1 Allele2 Effect StdErr `P-value` Direction
##   <int>    <int> <chr>       <chr>   <chr>    <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>    
## 1    20 35384250 20:35384250 T       C       0.0573  0.006  2.57e-21 +        
## # ℹ 7 more variables: HetISq <dbl>, HetChiSq <dbl>, HetDf <dbl>, HetPVal <dbl>,
## #   Direction1 <chr>, Direction2 <chr>, DirectionsMatch <lgl>
top_chr = top_snp[1,"chr"] %>% unlist
top_pos = top_snp[1,"pos"] %>% unlist

# make a forest plot for the top SNP across the two studies
top_snp_s1 = s1 %>% 
  filter(
    CHR == top_chr & POS == top_pos
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    STUDY = "Study 1"
  ) %>% 
  select(STUDY, BETA, SE)
  
top_snp_s2 = s2 %>% 
  filter(
    CHR == top_chr & POS == top_pos
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    STUDY = "Study 2"
  ) %>% 
  select(STUDY, BETA, SE)

top_snp = top_snp %>%
  mutate(
    STUDY = "META",
    BETA = Effect,
    SE = StdErr
  ) %>% 
  select(STUDY, BETA, SE)

meta.results = rbind(
  top_snp,
  top_snp_s1,
  top_snp_s2
) %>% 
  mutate(
    l95 = BETA -1.96 * SE,
    u95 = BETA + 1.96 * SE
  )

meta.results %>% 
  ggplot() +
  aes(
    y = fct_rev(STUDY)
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(
      x = BETA,
      size = factor(STUDY == "META"),
      shape = factor(STUDY == "META")
    )
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    aes(
      xmin = l95,
      xmax = u95
    )
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  scale_size_manual(values = c(2,7)) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(16,18)) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.line.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y= element_blank(),
    axis.title.y= element_blank()
  )

We used an Inverse-Variance Based approach to perform meta-analysis which also accounted for corrections on population stratification and unaccounted relatedness and that checked for homogeneity across our effect sizes. Across the two studies, we have 66,385 shared variants and 453,161 variants only identified in one of the studies. Of the shared variants, only about 50.2% of them share their direction of effect. Calculating the number of unique variants to each study individually lead to 38,093 in study 1 and 415,095 in study 2 (though note that these sum to 453,188, assuming there the missing 27 variants were potentially dropped in generating the merged meta-analysis data with METAL). The most significant variant shared across both studies was 20:35384250:T>C (p-value: 2.574e-21). Based on the forest plot generated for the top SNP, we can see that these studies are clearly not homogenous for this SNP. Study 1 has a huge standard error and a positive effect size that is dwarfing study 2.
